claim.observation: Volatility3 windows.netscan output contains an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 row with local endpoint 172.16.7.11:3262 and foreign endpoint 172.16.5.50:56722 (PID/Owner fields shown as '-'). The same netscan output also contains a LISTENING TCPv4 row on 0.0.0.0:3262 owned by PID 12528 subject_srv.ex.
claim.interpretation: There is an active (ESTABLISHED) TCP session whose local endpoint is 172.16.7.11 port 3262 — the same port on which subject_srv.ex (PID 12528) is LISTENING in the same output — with internal peer 172.16.5.50:56722. This indicates an inbound session from host 172.16.5.50 to the subject_srv.ex listener on the workstation (172.16.7.11) at acquisition time. The connection record does not bind a PID (Owner '-'), so attribution to subject_srv.ex is by local-port correlation rather than direct process ownership; 172.16.5.50 is the apparent remote peer of the backdoor.
